Forced to Eat Mango (2): MongoDB Basics in the Terminal

Naturally, the first thing to do is get familiar with some of MongoDB's basic commands. And honestly, a lot of the time we don't jump straight into a GUI — we get to know a few basic commands through the terminal first. Once you're comfortable with those, going back to a GUI makes you really appreciate how nice the GUI is.
By default, MongoDB ships with a few databases for you to test and play with, so let's start by getting familiar with some basic database operations.
to see which databases currently exist.
You'll notice every database has a
next to it — that's just how much storage the database is using.
If you want to check which database you're currently working in, type
. In my case I'm in the
So what if you want to switch to a different database? It's pretty simple — just type
. Once you do, you'll get a message back saying
, meaning you've switched over to the
But here's a fun thing about switching databases: even if that database doesn't exist, you can still switch right into it. For example, I don't currently have an

So when you switch to a database that doesn't exist, MongoDB automatically creates it for you. Once it's created, if you don't add any data, the moment you switch away it gets released again — which is why it won't show up in
. You can think of it like working with objects in JavaScript: you can reference a property name with the dot operator, but if you don't actually assign anything to it, nothing gets recorded.
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
So far we've only learned how to switch databases. But if you want to see the data inside a database, you use
. I'd recommend switching to one of the default databases first — like
, otherwise you'll just get a blank result.
If you map collections back to a SQL database, they're basically the equivalent of Tables.
One last thing worth mentioning: MongoDB is essentially JavaScript under the hood, so you can actually type some JavaScript right here in the shell.

Before touching any fancy GUI, it's worth getting comfortable with MongoDB straight from the terminal. This post walks through the basic commands — listing databases, switching between them, showing collections — and the slightly weird thing where switching to a database that doesn't exist just works. Once you know these basics, the GUI tools will feel a lot nicer.
